museum.dek: May 2003: The Wind Waker reaches Europe in May.

items.title: PS2 remains dominant in UK high-street space, but Xbox Live gives Microsoft a clear identity.
items.body: A year-level context marker included to frame the month without claiming a new event happened on a specific day.

items.title: GBA SP makes handheld hardware feel fashionable as well as practical.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: The Legend of Zelda: The Wind Waker
items.body: Nintendo's cel-shaded sea becomes an argument first and a classic later.
items.sourceNote: Regional dates vary; Europe received the game in May 2003.

items.title: Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic
items.body: BioWare makes a Star Wars RPG that feels authored, moral and enormous.
items.sourceNote: Xbox timing is widely placed in July 2003; PC follows later.

items.title: EyeToy: Play
items.body: A camera turns the living room into a party interface, especially visible in UK retail.
items.sourceNote: European launch timing is commonly placed in July 2003; verify exact UK date before final copy.

items.title: Soulcalibur II
items.body: A fighting game becomes a multi-console comparison object through guest characters.
items.sourceNote: Late summer 2003 timing varies by region and platform.

items.title: Viewtiful Joe
items.body: Capcom's comic-book action gives GameCube one of its sharp cult objects.
items.sourceNote: 2003 timing varies by region; September/October is commonly cited.

items.title: Call of Duty
items.body: Infinity Ward turns PC war shooters into a scripted ensemble spectacle.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for October 2003.

items.title: Max Payne 2: The Fall of Max Payne
items.body: Remedy makes noir, physics and melodrama feel beautifully over-serious.
items.sourceNote: Initial PC release is widely placed in October 2003.

items.title: Prince of Persia: The Sands of Time
items.body: Ubisoft rewinds the platformer into something graceful and tactile.
items.sourceNote: Sources place release in November 2003.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, buying and industry context for the month and its wider year.

items.title: Game Boy Advance SP launches in Europe
items.body: Nintendo's lit clamshell handheld becomes a desirable object as much as a revision.
items.sourceNote: SPOnG/Nintendo Europe material lists March 28, 2003.

items.title: Steam launches
items.body: Valve's client arrives as an update mechanism before it becomes the centre of PC ownership.
items.sourceNote: PC Gamer and Steam references list September 12, 2003.

items.title: Nokia N-Gage launches
items.body: A phone-console hybrid reaches shops before mobile hardware and habits are ready for it.
items.sourceNote: Nokia's Q3 2003 release notes sales beginning October 7.

items.title: Xbox Live matures
items.body: Voice chat, friends lists and online shooters give Xbox an identity beyond power.
items.sourceNote: Year-level Xbox Live context; verify individual service milestones before final copy.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How the network felt around the edges of play.

items.title: Forums and files
items.body: Online life is forums, GameFAQs, IRC, MSN Messenger, fan sites, clan pages and demo downloads that take planning.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Services and servers
items.body: Steam appears first as a nuisance to some PC players, especially around updates and account ownership.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Voice and identity
items.body: Xbox Live turns the headset into a new living-room object, making voice both thrilling and annoying.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Everyday connection
items.body: Broadband is spreading, but UK households still understand downloads, patches and online play through patience.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A curator's narrative sketch of the month as lived culture.

items.title: The room
items.body: It felt like gaming was becoming connected in several incompatible ways at once: Xbox headsets, Steam accounts, phone games and handheld revisions.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The shelf
items.body: The PS2 was still the safe centre of the room, but the interesting edges were multiplying.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The conversation
items.body: GBA SP made handheld play feel sleek; N-Gage made it feel awkwardly futuristic; Steam made PC gaming feel like it had acquired a front door.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The afterimage
items.body: For UK players, the experience was still filtered through demo discs, Friday high-street visits, import screenshots and whatever the home broadband could manage.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.
